message.content:
Repo: /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay (branch harden/pass-1). Venv (run PoCs): /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/.venv/bin/python.
This is a dataset-agnostic ML/methodology engine being hardened for PRODUCTION. Hard constraints (do NOT flag as bugs): local-first/data-sovereign (loopback only, nothing leaves the box), no metered API (subscription OAuth only), engine never imports adapters/datasets (ADR-0002), optional heavy backends are lazy-imported (ADR-0006). Read real code; prefer findings you can back with a concrete repro/PoC against the venv. Report ONLY genuine production-readiness defects (not style/nits). Each finding needs a real file path and, where possible, a line.

You are independent VERIFIER #1 of 2. A prior adversarial assessor reported this production-readiness finding:

TITLE: mypy is pinned to `python_version = "3.11"` but CI advertises and matrix-tests up to 3.14, so 3.12–3.14-only type errors are never caught
DIMENSION: ops, packaging & supply chain
CLAIMED SEVERITY: LOW
FILE: pyproject.toml 87
DESCRIPTION: `[tool.mypy] python_version = "3.11"` forces mypy to type-check the code as if running on Python 3.11 regardless of the runner's interpreter. The core CI lane runs mypy across a 3.11–3.14 matrix and the integration lane runs it on 3.12, but because the analysis target is fixed at 3.11, type behavior that differs across versions (e.g. stdlib stubs that gained/changed signatures in 3.12+, version-gated typing constructs) is only ever validated at the 3.11 floor. The project classifies and matrix-tests 3.14 as supported, so the type checker should also exercise the newer targets to make that support real rather than asserted.
SUGGESTED FIX: Either remove the fixed `python_version` so mypy infers the target from the running interpreter (letting the matrix exercise each version), or run an explicit mypy invocation per matrix Python (`mypy --python-version ${{ matrix.python-version }}`). Keep the lazy-backend overrides as-is.

Independently determine whether this is a TRUE production-readiness problem. Read the actual code; attempt a concrete PoC against the venv if applicable. Set is_real=false if it is a false positive, already-handled, or merely a documented/justified design choice given the hard constraints. Recalibrate severity. Provide your evidence (what you read/ran).

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

---
total 8

1	name: ci
2	
3	on:
4	  push:
5	    branches: [main]
6	  pull_request:
7	
8	jobs:
9	  # Dependency-free core: the engine must lint, type-check, and test with NO extras installed
10	  # (optional backends are import-guarded; integration tests skip). This guards ADR-0006.
11	  core:
12	    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
13	    strategy:
14	      matrix:
15	        python-version: ["3.11", "3.12", "3.13", "3.14"]
16	    steps:
17	      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
18	      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
19	        with:
20	          python-version: ${{ matrix.python-version }}
21	      - name: Install (no extras)
22	        run: |
23	          python -m pip install --upgrade pip
24	          pip install -e ".[dev]"
25	      - name: Lint (ruff)
26	        run: ruff check src tests
27	      - name: Format check (ruff)
28	        run: ruff format --check src tests
29	      - name: Type-check (mypy)
30	        run: mypy
31	      - name: Test (pytest)
32	        run: pytest -q
33	
34	  # Extras lane: install the real backends and run the integration suite so the backend code
35	  # paths actually execute, and mypy type-checks against the REAL (not ignored) library types.
36	  # Service-dependent tests (ollama/qdrant/postgres) skip when the service is absent in CI; the
37	  # extra-only tests (langgraph graph, mlflow lifecycle, OTel, numpy primitives, methodology
38	  # e2e) run for real.
39	  integration:
40	    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
41	    services:
42	      postgres:
43	        image: postgres:16
44	        env:
45	          POSTGRES_PASSWORD: postgres
46	          POSTGRES_DB: assay
47	        ports: ["5432:5432"]
48	        options: >-
49	          --health-cmd "pg_isready -U postgres"
50	          --health-interval 5s --health-timeout 5s --health-retries 10
51	      qdrant:
52	        image: qdrant/qdrant:v1.12.4
53	        ports: ["6333:6333"]
54	    env:
55	      # loopback host keeps the data-sovereignty guard satisfied; creds are redacted on error
56	      ASSAY_TEST_POSTGRES_URL: postgresql://postgres:postgres@localhost:5432/assay
57	    steps:
58	      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
59	      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
60	        with:
61	          python-version: "3.12"
62	      - name: Install (all extras)
63	        run: |
64	          python -m pip install --upgrade pip
65	          pip install -e ".[dev,reasoning,observability,persistence,orchestration,baseline]"
66	      - name: Type-check against real backend types (mypy)
67	        run: mypy
68	      # Real backends now run: langgraph graph + interrupt/resume, mlflow lifecycle, OTel,
69	      # numpy primitives, the T3 drain/classification (synthetic SDK, no metered call), AND —
70	      # via the service containers — the live Postgres checkpointer + Qdrant vector store.
71	      # Ollama and the metered frontier tier are not in CI and skip honestly.
72	      - name: Integration + unit tests with coverage
73	        run: pytest -q --cov=assay_engine --cov-report=term-missing
74

content.text: The finding is factually accurate about the configuration. Let me verify the actual claim about whether `python_version = "3.11"` actually suppresses version-specific type errors by running a concrete PoC against the venv.

input.description: Check venv python and mypy versions

content.text: The venv is Python 3.14 with mypy installed. Let me run a concrete PoC to test whether the pinned `python_version = "3.11"` actually masks version-gated type errors.

input.command:
cd /tmp && mkdir -p mypy_poc && cd /tmp/mypy_poc && cat > demo.py <<'EOF'
import sys

# Version-gated typing construct: tomllib only exists in 3.11+ stdlib,
# but let's use something gated at 3.12+ in typeshed.
from typing import override  # typing.override added in 3.12

class Base:
    def f(self) -> int:
        return 1

class Child(Base):
    @override
    def f(self) -> int:
        return 2
EOF
echo "=== mypy with python_version=3.11 ==="
/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/.venv/bin/mypy --python-version 3.11 --strict demo.py
echo "exit: $?"
echo "=== mypy with python_version=3.14 ==="
/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/.venv/bin/mypy --python-version 3.14 --strict demo.py
echo "exit: $?"
input.description: PoC testing version-gated typing.override across mypy targets

demo.py:12: error: Untyped decorator makes function "f" untyped  [untyped-decorator]
Found 2 errors in 1 file (checked 1 source file)
exit: 1

content.text: This PoC demonstrates the *opposite direction* of the claim: pinning to 3.11 is actually *stricter* (flags `typing.override` as unavailable). Let me test the direction the finding actually claims: code that is fine on 3.11 but breaks on 3.12-3.14, that would be missed by pinning to 3.11.

EOF

content.text: The codebase uses only stable, broadly-available typing constructs (`Any`, `Callable`, `Mapping`, `Protocol`, `TypeVar`, etc.) — all available since 3.9/3.10 and stable across 3.11–3.14. There are no version-gated constructs (no `typing.override`, no `Self` via typing, no constructs removed in 3.14). Let me confirm the actual mypy run on the real code passes and check whether running per-matrix-version would surface anything.

input.command: cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && echo "=== default (pinned 3.11) ===" && /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/.venv/bin/mypy 2>&1 | tail -3; echo "=== forced 3.14 ===" && /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/.venv/bin/mypy --python-version 3.14 2>&1 | tail -3

content.text:
Both targets pass identically on the real codebase. My analysis is complete.

Key findings from my independent verification:

1. **Factually accurate config claim**: `pyproject.toml:87` does pin `python_version = "3.11"`, CI matrix-tests 3.11–3.14 (`ci.yml:15`), and classifiers advertise through 3.14 (`pyproject.toml:21-24`). The mechanical description is correct.

2. **The claimed risk direction is largely backwards / overstated**: My PoC showed that pinning to a *lower* version (3.11) is generally *more conservative* for typing — e.g. `typing.override` is flagged as unavailable at 3.11 but accepted at 3.14. The finding's premise that newer-only errors get masked is the less common direction. The genuine case it could catch (constructs valid on 3.11 but *removed* in 3.14, like `typing.ByteString`) didn't even reproduce in my PoC — typeshed/mypy didn't flag it at either target.

3. **Zero actual impact on this codebase**: The source uses only stable, broadly-available typing constructs. Real mypy runs at both `3.11` and `3.14` pass identically (40 files, no issues). There is no version-gated code to mis-validate.

4. **Real but minor process gap**: It is still a legitimate ops observation that the type-check target is fixed and doesn't track the matrix, so the "support" for 3.12–3.14 is asserted-but-not-type-validated. This is a real-but-low defect — a hardening improvement, not a production blocker. The suggested fix (`mypy --python-version ${{ matrix.python-version }}`) is sound.

This is a real-but-LOW process/hygiene observation with no demonstrable current defect.

input.reasoning:
The configuration facts are accurate: pyproject.toml:87 pins [tool.mypy] python_version = "3.11"; .github/workflows/ci.yml:15 matrix-tests 3.11-3.14 in the core lane and 3.12 in the integration lane (ci.yml:61); pyproject classifiers advertise 3.11-3.14 (pyproject.toml:21-24, requires-python >=3.11,<3.15). So mypy always analyzes against the 3.11 target regardless of the runner interpreter, meaning version-specific typing behavior across 3.12-3.14 is not exercised by the type checker even though those versions are claimed as supported. That makes the finding a genuine ops/hygiene gap.

However, the severity and the claimed risk are overstated. My PoC shows pinning to the LOWER version (3.11) is generally the MORE conservative/strict choice for typing: typing.override is flagged as unavailable at --python-version 3.11 but accepted at 3.14 (opposite of the finding's "newer-only errors are masked" premise). The one genuine direction it could catch (constructs valid on 3.11 but removed in 3.14, e.g. typing.ByteString) did not even reproduce — mypy/typeshed flagged nothing at either target. Most decisively, the actual codebase only uses stable typing constructs (Any, Callable, Mapping, Protocol, TypeVar, runtime_checkable; no typing.override, no removed-in-3.14 names; one guarded TYPE_CHECKING block), and real mypy runs pass identically at both 3.11 and 3.14 (40 files, no issues). There is therefore no current defect, only the theoretical possibility that a future version-gated construct slips through. The suggested fix (mypy --python-version ${{ matrix.python-version }} per matrix entry) is correct and cheap, so this remains a valid low-severity hardening item rather than a false positive or a production blocker.
input.evidence: Read pyproject.toml lines 1-50 and 75-109 (confirmed python_version="3.11" at line 87, classifiers 3.11-3.14 at 21-24, requires-python >=3.11,<3.15 at line 10). Read .github/workflows/ci.yml in full (core matrix ["3.11","3.12","3.13","3.14"] line 15, integration pinned "3.12" line 61, both run bare `mypy`). venv is Python 3.14.5 with mypy 2.1.0. PoC in /tmp/mypy_poc: (1) typing.override -> ERROR at --python-version 3.11, SUCCESS at 3.14 (shows 3.11 pin is stricter, opposite of claim); (2) typing.ByteString and asyncio.get_event_loop -> no error at either target. grep of src/ typing imports: only stable constructs, one TYPE_CHECKING guard (methodology/adjudication.py:37), no version-gated names. Real `mypy` (pinned 3.11) and `mypy --python-version 3.14` both report "Success: no issues found in 40 source files" -> no current divergence.

content.text: I've completed my independent verification. The finding is real but LOW severity — see the structured output above for full reasoning and evidence.
